key_points: Dr. Habiba Alsafar (Dean, CMHS) asked whether BSCND + Sports Medicine Minor qualifies graduates for UAE fitness-coach / personal-trainer licensure
Her original inquiry (May 13, 2026) forwarded by Kinda Khalaf to Kartik Rangaraj and Sara Almessabi for response
Kinda Khalaf confirmed courses were preliminary proposals by Dr. Carlo, not formally reviewed by committee
Kinda suggested submitting new concentration alongside main BSCND program
Kinda noted benchmarking against leading programs and possibly ~18 credits total for strengthened minor
full_text_excerpt: Re: Bachelor of Science (B.Sc.) in Clinical Nutrition and Dietetics with a Minor in Sports Medicine. From Habiba Al Safar (Dean, CMHS). Date Thu 2026-05-14. Dr. Habiba asked: whether the current program qualifies graduates to obtain a license or certification to work as fitness coaches or personal trainers, including designing exercise programs, supervising workouts, and teaching stretching and conditioning techniques. Kinda Khalaf (Assoc Dean UG) replied that courses shared earlier were preliminary proposals drafted by Dr. Carlo today, based on her email and the example provided. They are not part of the original sports minor, which remains on hold pending program accreditation. The committee has not yet formally developed these courses. Kinda recommended proceeding by refining the minor in alignment with this direction and submitting it alongside the main BSCND program. She also noted benchmarking against leading programs and potentially strengthening the minor to approximately 18 credits.

key_points: Dr. Habiba expressed satisfaction with the detailed response from Sara Saleh
Sara Saleh's response clarified the program may support graduates in pursuing internationally recognized certifications (NASM, ACE, ISSA, ACSM, NSCA-CSCS, REPs UAE)
Sara confirmed no dedicated practical coaching or fitness-training component currently exists in the Sports Medicine Minor
Program currently supports clinical pathway (DoH Dietitian) and broadens non-clinical pathways
Additional practical training, CPR/First Aid, and fitness-specific components still needed for full certification eligibility
full_text_excerpt: From: Habiba Al Safar (Dean, CMHS). Date: Thu 2026-05-14 7:32 PM. Dr. Habiba replied: Thank you very much for your detailed and thoughtful response. You have fully answered my inquiries and provided the clarification I needed regarding the certification pathways and the relationship between the proposed program and internationally recognized fitness and sports performance certifications. Sara Saleh's previous response stated: The proposed program may support graduates in pursuing internationally recognized certifications/examinations such as NASM, ACE, ISSA, ACSM, NSCA-CSCS, and REPs UAE-related pathways. However, there is currently no dedicated practical coaching, exercise prescription, or fitness-training component specifically tailored toward fitness coach or personal trainer certification pathways within the Sports Medicine Minor itself. Additional practical training, coaching practicum exposure, CPR/First Aid certification, and fitness-specific training components may still be needed.

key_points: Kartik Rangaraj (Period Director, MedEd 1,2) presented proposed new SPMD courses table (SPMD305-308) to Dr. Habiba

Table shows 4 proposed courses: SPMD305 Personal Training & Exercise Programme Design (Y3 Fall, 2L+1Lab), SPMD306 Group Fitness Stretching & Conditioning Instruction (Y3 Spring, 1L+2Lab), SPMD307 Fitness Coaching Behaviour Change & Business of Fitness (Y4 Fall, 3L), SPMD308 Fitness Coaching Practicum & REPs UAE Certification Capstone (Y4 Spring, 200 hrs)
Dr. Habiba asked Kartik to confirm whether proposed courses were already embedded in curriculum and whether completion would meet fitness coach certification requirements
Kartik confirmed these are newly proposed additions under Dr. Kinda's guidance and Ms. Sara Almessabi's contributions
Sara created shared Teams BSCND folder as repository for all BSCND docs submitted for review/approval
full_text_excerpt: From Habiba Al Safar (Dean, CMHS). Sent: Thursday, May 14, 2026 10:55 AM. To: Kartik Rangaraj. Subject: Re: Bachelor of Science (B.Sc.) in Clinical Nutrition and Dietetics with a Minor in Sports Medicine. Dear Dr. Carlo, Thank you for your email. After reviewing the Excel files, I came across the table below and wanted to clarify whether these courses would enable students enrolled in the program to qualify as certified fitness coaches. Could you please confirm whether these courses are already embedded within the curriculum and whether completion of these courses would meet the requirements for fitness coach certification? Proposed New SPMD Courses — Sports & Fitness Coaching Concentration: SPMD305 Personal Training & Exercise Programme Design (3 cr, 2 lec + 1 lab, Y3 Fall, prereq SPMD301); SPMD306 Group Fitness Stretching & Conditioning Instruction (3 cr, 1 lec + 2 lab, Y3 Spring, prereq SPMD302); SPMD307 Fitness Coaching Behaviour Change & Business of Fitness (3 cr, 3 lec, Y4 Fall, prereq SPMD305); SPMD308 Fitness Coaching Practicum & REPs UAE Certification Capstone (3 cr, 1 lec + 200 supervised hours + CPR/AED, Y4 Spring, prereq SPMD306+SPMD307).

key_points: SPMD301: Foundations of Sports Medicine, 3L/0Lab, 3 credits, Y3 Fall
Prerequisite: BIOL 207 Human Physiology
Textbook: Prentice 2021 Principles of Athletic Training (17th ed.)
Coverage: anatomy of movement, biomechanics, common sports injuries, exercise physiology principles, interdisciplinary athlete care
CLO1-CLO5 include: movement pattern analysis, physiological responses to exercise, sports injury analysis, interdisciplinary team roles, professional communication
Status: Pending UGCC Review
No lab hours; primarily didactic; athlete-centred framing
full_text_excerpt: SPMD301: Foundations of Sports Medicine (3 Lecture hours, 0 Laboratory hours, 3 Credits). Prerequisites: BIOL 207: Human Physiology. Course Catalog Description: Introduction to sports medicine concepts including anatomy of movement, biomechanics, common sports injuries, exercise physiology principles, and the interdisciplinary approach to athlete care tissue healing, injury mechanisms, and the roles of healthcare professionals within sports medicine teams. The course prepares nutrition students to collaborate effectively with sports medicine professionals. Textbook: Prentice, W. E. (2021). Principles of athletic training: A guide to evidence-based clinical practice (17th ed.). McGraw-Hill Education. CLO1: Analyze human movement patterns in athletic populations using anatomical and biomechanical principles. CLO2: Analyze physiological responses and adaptations associated with acute exercise. CLO3: Analyze common sports injuries with respect to mechanisms of injury, clinical presentation, and evidence-based treatment. CLO4: Evaluate the role of interdisciplinary sports medicine teams. CLO5: Demonstrate professional communication skills related to sports medicine concepts.

key_points: SPMD302: Exercise Physiology, 3L/0Lab, 3 credits, Y3 Spring
Prerequisite: BIOL 207 Human Physiology
Textbook: Kenney, Wilmore & Costill 2022 Physiology of Sport and Exercise (8th ed.); also ACSM Guidelines 11th ed.
Coverage: bioenergetics, cardiovascular/respiratory/metabolic/neuromuscular/endocrine adaptations, training principles, environmental physiology
CLO5 covers evidence-based training program design for performance goals — athlete-framed, not general population
Status: Pending UGCC Review
Foundation for understanding exercise-nutrition interaction
full_text_excerpt: SPMD302: Exercise Physiology (3 Lecture hours, 0 Laboratory hours, 3 Credits). Prerequisites: BIOL 207: Human Physiology. Course Catalog Description: Acute and chronic physiological responses to exercise including cardiovascular, respiratory, metabolic, neuromuscular, and endocrine adaptations bioenergetics, training principles, and environmental physiology as they relate to athletic performance and health. The course provides a foundation for understanding the interaction between exercise training and nutrition. Textbook: Kenney, W. L., Wilmore, J. H., & Costill, D. L. (2022). Physiology of sport and exercise (8th ed.). Human Kinetics. CLO1: Apply principles of bioenergetics to explanations of ATP production and energy system contributions. CLO2: Analyze cardiovascular, respiratory, neuromuscular, and endocrine adaptations. CLO3: Conduct exercise physiology assessments including VO2max testing and body composition analysis. CLO4: Evaluate effects of environmental conditions on exercise performance. CLO5: Design evidence-based training programs to optimize physiological adaptations for specific performance goals.

key_points: SPMD303: Injury Prevention & Rehabilitation, 3L/0Lab, 3 credits, Y3 Spring
Prerequisite: BMED 301 (typo — should be SPMD301)
Textbook: Tyler & McHugh 2020 Sports Injury Prevention and Rehabilitation
Coverage: screening, injury risk assessment, prevention program design, rehabilitation progressions, return-to-play
CLO2: design injury prevention programs integrating strength, flexibility, neuromuscular control — injury-frame only
Status: Pending UGCC Review
Emphasis on nutrition role in tissue healing and interdisciplinary collaboration
full_text_excerpt: SPMD303: Injury Prevention & Rehabilitation (3 Lecture hours, 0 Laboratory hours, 3 Credits). Prerequisites: BMED 301: Foundations of Sports Medicine. Course Catalog Description: Principles of sports injury prevention and evidence-based rehabilitation approaches screening methods, injury risk assessment, prevention program design, and rehabilitation progressions for common athletic injuries. The course emphasizes interdisciplinary collaboration and the role of nutrition in tissue healing and return-to-play decision making. Textbook: Tyler, T. F., & McHugh, M. P. (2020). Sports injury prevention and rehabilitation. Routledge. CLO1: Apply evidence-based screening tools for identification of injury risk factors. CLO2: Design injury prevention programs integrating strength training, flexibility, neuromuscular control, and sport-specific conditioning. CLO3: Analyze healing processes and phases of tissue repair relevant to rehabilitation progression. CLO4: Evaluate therapeutic modalities and exercise interventions in sports injury rehabilitation. CLO5: Collaborate within return-to-play protocols.

key_points: SPMD304: Performance Assessment & Technology, 2L/1Lab, 3 credits, Y4 Fall
Prerequisite: BMED 302 (typo — should be SPMD302); only SPMD course with lab hours (150 min/wk)
Textbook: Haff & Triplett 2016 Essentials of Strength Training and Conditioning (4th ed.) — NSCA CSCS textbook
Coverage: biomechanical analysis, fitness testing, wearable technology, data analytics, athlete monitoring
CLO5: design technology-enhanced performance monitoring systems
Status: Pending UGCC Review; credits listed as 0 in _data.json (data entry error — should be 3)
Only SPMD course with lab; uses NSCA textbook but no CSCS certification pathway named
full_text_excerpt: SPMD304: Performance Assessment & Technology (2 Lecture hours, 1 Laboratory hour, 3 Credits). Prerequisites: BMED 302: Exercise Physiology. Course Catalog Description: Assessment methods and emerging technologies for evaluating athletic performance. Students gain hands-on experience with biomechanical analysis, fitness testing, wearable technology, and data analytics for athlete monitoring. Emphasis is placed on performance assessment, data interpretation, and integration of technology into sports science and nutrition practice. Textbook: Haff, G. G., & Triplett, N. T. (2016). Essentials of strength training and conditioning (4th ed.). Human Kinetics. CLO1: Apply biomechanical analysis techniques including force platforms and motion capture. CLO2: Conduct comprehensive fitness assessments using standardized protocols for strength, power, speed, agility, and endurance. CLO3: Analyze performance data through statistical methods for training recommendations. CLO4: Evaluate emerging athlete-monitoring technologies including wearable sensors, GPS tracking, and HRV monitors. CLO5: Design technology-enhanced performance monitoring systems integrating physiological, biomechanical, and training load data.

key_points: Comprehensive curriculum-map analysis vs. UAE fitness-coach licensure requirements (version 1.0, 2026-05-14)
Bottom line: current BSCND + SPMD Minor does NOT qualify graduates for REPs UAE registration
6 UAE regulatory bodies documented: REPs UAE, ADSC, DSC, GAS, DoH, MoHRE
12 REPs UAE requirements gap-analyzed against current curriculum; only Sports Nutrition (NUTR305) is fully covered
Option A recommended: 4 new courses SPMD305-308 adding 12 credits, total rises 124→136
Approval path: 7 steps from CMHS Dean endorsement through REPs UAE ICREPs accreditation
Physical Manager PPTX (Cseh 2018) benchmark mapped — sport-specific practicals and MSc-level physio content out of scope

key_points: Cseh 2018 benchmark: 3-level Physical Manager program (BSc 6 semesters, Intermediate 8 semesters, MSc 10 semesters)

Level 1 BSc covers: anatomy of bones/muscles/joints/nerves/movements, physiology, gymnastics/swimming/athletics/ball sports (practical), CPR/first aid, training theory, nutrition science, weight & equipment training, internship 8 wks/semester
Level 2 Intermediate adds: teaching theory, psychology, pedagogy, wellness science, individual and group teaching practicals
Level 3 MSc adds: internal medicine, neurology, orthopedics, pediatrics, rheumatology, cardiology, gerontology, manual therapies, physical therapy — out of scope for BSCND UG
Career opportunities: fitness centers, private sector, schools, army/police, special needs centers, worksite fitness, medical centers, professional sport clubs
BSCND scope mapping: anatomy/physiology covered by BIOL 111/112; practical sport modules out of scope; SPMD305 covers weight/equipment training; SPMD308 covers CPR/first aid
full_text_excerpt: PHYSICAL MANAGER PROGRAM PLAN 2018 KRISZTINA CSEH. Physical Manager: Can be a new brand for physical health science. XXI century professionals with the latest knowledge. Science based profession. Takes the whole person approach to health and wellbeing. Level 1 BSc Physical Manager — 1st Semester: anatomy of bones (4 classes/wk, 3 theory + 1 practical), physiology (4/wk), basic gymnastic (4/wk), basic swimming (4/wk), basic athletics (4/wk), basic ball sports (4/wk), CPR/first aid (2/wk), training theory (4/wk), nutrition science (2/wk), Internship: 8 weeks during semester. 5th/6th Semester adds: weight trainings (4/wk, 2 theory + 2 practical), equipment trainings (4/wk). Level 2: teaching theory, psychology, pedagogy, wellness science, individual teaching, group teaching. Level 3 MSc: internal medicine, neurology, orthopedy, pediatry, reumatology, cardiology, gerontology, manual therapies, physical therapy — Admission: Intermediate Physical Manager.

key_points: 5 BSCND PLOs: PLO1 Problem-Solving with Science, PLO2 System/Program Design, PLO3 Experimentation & Data Analysis, PLO4 Communication & Collaborative Practice, PLO5 Ethics & Professional Responsibility

15 KU ILOs covering breadth of knowledge, scientific method, Arabic culture, sustainability, communication, digital literacy, research, critical thinking, innovation, lifelong learning, teamwork, diversity, ethics, self-management
SPMD304 has credits: 0 in this file — confirmed data entry error; actual 3 credits per syllabus docx
SPMD303 and SPMD304 have BMED code typos in prerequisites (BMED 301/302 should be SPMD 301/302)

key_points: Formal ADEK Authorization of New Higher Education Program submission for BSCND
Khalifa University: ranked 177th globally QS 2026, 37th THE Asia 2025, top UAE university 8 consecutive years
Lists 17 submission documents per ADEK criteria; QFEmirates Level 6 program
Abbreviations list confirms ADSC, DOH, UGCC, PLO, CLO, QFEmirates, ACEND framing
Program prepares for DoH Dietitian licensure (Bachelor + 2 yrs exp + DoH exam)
Key bodies cited: ADEK, DoH, ADPHC, SEHA, CCAD, EFAD, ACEND, BDA
full_text_excerpt: Khalifa University seeks authorization for the proposed Bachelor of Science in Clinical Nutrition and Dietetics (BSCND) program, which aligns with national priorities and KU's commitment to advancing research-informed undergraduate education. Khalifa University is ranked 177th globally in the QS World University Rankings 2026 and 37th in the Times Higher Education Asia University Rankings 2025, maintaining its position among the top 200 universities worldwide. KU has remained the top university in the UAE for eight consecutive years. Table of Abbreviations includes: ACEND - Accreditation Council for Education in Nutrition and Dietetics; ADEK - Abu Dhabi Department of Education and Knowledge; ADSC - Abu Dhabi Sports Council; BSCND - Bachelor of Science in Clinical Nutrition and Dietetics; DOH - Department of Health (Abu Dhabi); PLO - Program Learning Outcome; QFEmirates - Qualifications Framework Emirates; UGCC - Undergraduate Curriculum Committee. List of Submitted Documents includes 17 items from Program Authorization Application through Additional Documents (Surveys, MoUs, Advisory Board Information).
